nodes.content:
Essay thesis

Camus asks whether life can be lived without appeal to eternal meaning. His answer is neither resignation nor suicide, but lucid revolt: live with the absurd, without false consolation.

> There is but one truly serious philosophical problem, and that is suicide.

> The struggle itself toward the heights is enough to fill a man’s heart.
nodes.source_text: There is but one truly serious philosophical problem, and that is suicide.

nodes.content: The central question
The absurd condition
Temptations and refusals
Living without appeal
Sisyphus as image

Starting question

Camus begins with the existential question beneath all others: if life has no final meaning, should one keep living? The essay tests whether suicide follows from absurdity.
nodes.source_text: Judging whether life is or is not worth living amounts to answering the fundamental question of philosophy.

nodes.content:
The hunger for clarity

Human beings seek unity, reason, and explanation. The mind wants the world to answer it.
nodes.source_text: The mind’s deepest desire... is an insistence upon familiarity, an appetite for clarity.

nodes.content:
The world’s silence

The world does not satisfy that hunger. The absurd arises not from humanity alone or the world alone, but from their confrontation.

> The absurd is born of this confrontation between the human need and the unreasonable silence of the world.
nodes.source_text: The absurd is born of this confrontation.

nodes.content:
Absurdity is a relation

The absurd must be preserved as a tension. If either side is abolished - the human demand or the world’s silence - the absurd disappears.
nodes.source_text: The absurd depends as much on man as on the world.

nodes.content:
Proposal: physical suicide

Pro: if life lacks ultimate meaning, one response is to end it. Suicide seems to confess that life is not worth the effort.
nodes.source_text: Suicide is prepared within the silence of the heart.

nodes.content:
Camus’ objection to suicide

Con: suicide does not solve the absurd; it abolishes one of its terms. It is a surrender rather than a lucid answer. The task is to live with the contradiction.
nodes.source_text: Living is keeping the absurd alive.

nodes.content:
Proposal: philosophical suicide

Pro: another escape is the leap - religion, transcendence, or metaphysics that converts absurdity into hidden meaning. Kierkegaard, Jaspers, and others are treated as examples of this temptation.
nodes.source_text: The leap does not represent an extreme danger, as Kierkegaard would like it to do. The danger... lies in the subtle instant that precedes the leap.

nodes.content:
Camus’ objection to the leap

Con: the leap betrays the starting point. It claims to accept the absurd but then smuggles in meaning, hope, or reconciliation. Camus wants fidelity to what is known, not consolation by what is wished.
nodes.source_text: I do not want to found anything on the incomprehensible.

nodes.content:
Proposal: hope as remedy

Pro: one might endure life by hoping for another world, future redemption, historical completion, or eventual explanation.
nodes.source_text: Hope of another life one must deserve, or trickery of those who live not for life itself but for some great idea.

nodes.content:
Camus’ objection to hope

Con: hope can become evasion when it makes present life subordinate to a promised meaning. Camus asks for life without appeal.
nodes.source_text: What counts is not the best living but the most living.

nodes.content:
First consequence: revolt

To live absurdly is to keep saying no to false reconciliation while continuing to live. Revolt is constant lucidity.

> The absurd man can only drain everything to the bitter end, and deplete himself.
nodes.source_text: Revolt gives life its value.

nodes.content:
Second consequence: freedom

Without eternal purpose, the absurd person is released from the demand to justify life by a final end. Freedom becomes present-tense and concrete.
nodes.source_text: The absurd man feels released from everything outside that passionate attention crystallizing in him.

nodes.content:
Third consequence: passion

Camus does not recommend despairing withdrawal. Since there is no final appeal, the point is intensity, multiplicity, and lucid experience.
nodes.source_text: The present and the succession of presents before a constantly conscious soul is the ideal of the absurd man.

nodes.content:
Don Juan

Don Juan represents quantity and lucidity rather than eternal love. He does not seek final possession; he multiplies experience while knowing its limits.
nodes.source_text: It is not through lack of love that Don Juan goes from woman to woman.

nodes.content:
The actor

The actor lives many lives in a finite span, making visible the absurd compression of existence into roles, gestures, and endings.
nodes.source_text: The actor has three hours to be Iago or Alceste, Phaedra or Gloucester.

nodes.content:
The conqueror and creator

The conqueror and artist act without appeal to eternity. Creation does not explain the world but repeats and gives form to experience.
nodes.source_text: Creating is living doubly.

nodes.content:
Sisyphus’ punishment

Sisyphus is condemned to endless useless labor: rolling the stone up the mountain only to watch it fall. He is the mythic image of absurd repetition.
nodes.source_text: The gods had condemned Sisyphus to ceaselessly rolling a rock to the top of a mountain.

nodes.content:
The lucid descent

The decisive moment is not the upward strain but the descent, when Sisyphus knows the whole truth of his condition. Consciousness transforms punishment into defiance.
nodes.source_text: It is during that return, that pause, that Sisyphus interests me.

nodes.content:
One must imagine him happy

Camus’ final claim is that lucidity, revolt, and ownership of fate can make even an absurd life sufficient. Sisyphus has no hope, but he has his rock and his consciousness.

> One must imagine Sisyphus happy.
nodes.source_text: One must imagine Sisyphus happy.
